text.title: Overview and core principle
text.summary: The orchestrator coordinates phase-specific subagents by handing each one a path to a versioned prompt file plus a small parameter block, instead of re-typing instructions inline.

rule.statement: The orchestrator MUST NOT inline long instructions into spawn messages. It hands the subagent a path to a prompt file in `prompts/` and a small key-value parameter block.
rule.rationale: Behaviour drifts when the same instructions are paraphrased every run. Versioned prompt files make each phase a deterministic capability and let prompts be reviewed, diffed, and improved over time.

rule.statement: Each subagent prompt file MUST be self-contained — it briefs the subagent like a colleague who has not seen the parent conversation.
rule.rationale: Subagents do not inherit the parent's context window. A prompt that assumes shared state silently degrades into vague instructions.

section.title: Role split
section.p: The orchestrator does three things only: decides which phase runs next, decides what can run in parallel, and confirms sensitive actions (push, deploy, destructive ops) with the user. Everything else — analysis, planning, edits, builds, reviews, screenshots, delivery — lives in subagents.

list.item: Orchestrator: composition, parallelism decisions, user confirmations.
Subagents: domain work behind a single prompt file each.
Reference docs: cross-cutting knowledge (git conventions, screenshot strategy, delivery channel auth).
Playbooks: per-delivery-surface specializations of the generic flow.

section.title: Task manager neutrality
section.p: The pattern assumes a ticket-based source-of-truth where each item has an ID, status, description, comments, attachments, and updatable status. Any of the following is a fit:
Concretize the chosen tracker in `reference/tracker-conventions.md` (status names, ID format, MCP/CLI access). The orchestrator never hard-codes a vendor name.

rule.statement: User-facing strings (clarification questions, delivery captions) use the user's working language. Code, commit messages, prompt files, and reference docs use English.
rule.rationale: Mixed-language code is hard to grep; mixed-language conversation is impolite. The split is consistent across runs because it is encoded in the prompt files.
